summary.brief: Marshall v. Dodds. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: In February 2010, Marshall was diagnosed with Waldenstrom's macroglobulinemia, also known as lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma, a rare type of blood cancer.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dodds first evaluated Marshall on July 16, 1999, two days after she was admitted to Roper Hospital for a persistent high fever.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On September 15, 2004, Marshall returned to Dodds for the first time since the 1999 visit, and during this appointment, Dodds reviewed a 24-hour urine test performed a month prior that revealed urine protein levels of 3.5 grams per day.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: One expert, Barry L. Singer, M.D., a specialist in oncology, testified Marshall likely had blood cancer in 2004, which would have been revealed then if a UPEP or SPEP test had been performed.
evidence_index.speaker: Barry L. Singer, M.D.

evidence_index.text: Luke opined Dodds was negligent in failing to recognize that the continued proteinuria could constitute cancer and failing to order UPEP and SPEP testing, which would have revealed cancerous protein.
evidence_index.speaker: Robert G. Luke, M.D.

evidence_index.text: The circuit court concluded Dodds' alleged misdiagnoses after February 7, 2005, were a continuation of his previous alleged misdiagnoses and were not distinct acts of negligence that could serve as new trigger points of the statute of repose.
evidence_index.speaker: Circuit court conclusion

evidence_index.text: Dr. Roane began treating Marshall in 2000 and in that year diagnosed Marshall with mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD), a rare autoimmune disease.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Zizic testified that from 2004 until treatment ended in 2007, Roane should have administered yearly laboratory studies until reaching a definitive diagnosis of Marshall's blood cancer.
evidence_index.speaker: Thomas M. Zizic, M.D.

evidence_index.text: The Marshalls focus solely upon the negligent conduct of Roane occurring on and after April 29, 2005, contending separate repose periods were triggered by each misdiagnosis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Section 15-3-545(A) begins to run after each occurrence, which is consistent with our rejection of the continuous treatment rule and the continuous tort doctrine.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient admitted to Roper Hospital with persistent high fever. Initial evaluation by Dr. Dodds revealed elevated sedimentation rates and proteinuria.

requestables.name: 24-hour urine test
requestables.description: Measurement of protein levels in urine over 24 hours

reveal.result_summary: Proteinuria with levels of 3.5 to 4.2 grams per day
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates significant proteinuria, potential marker for underlying disease
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt further testing to determine cause

requestables.name: UPEP and SPEP tests
requestables.description: Urine and serum protein electrophoresis tests to identify protein type

reveal.decision_impact: Failure to order these tests delayed diagnosis of cancer

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with persistent high fever and is admitted to hospital

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up and Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Patient undergoes follow-up visits with persistent proteinuria

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with persistent proteinuria and elevated sedimentation rates
decision_points.prompt: What further testing should be ordered to investigate the cause of proteinuria?

options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires ruling out cancerous protein in cases of significant proteinuria
Failed to address potential underlying causes of proteinuria

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant continued monitoring without additional tests

expected_action_court.description: UPEP and SPEP tests should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated these tests were necessary to diagnose cancer

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order necessary tests delayed diagnosis of cancer
explanation.what_should_have_happened: UPEP and SPEP tests should have been conducted to identify cancerous protein
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient diagnosed with Waldenstrom's macroglobulinemia in 2010

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to order UPEP and SPEP tests
Causation established - earlier diagnosis would have identified cancer

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to order UPEP and SPEP tests
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Tests would have identified cancerous protein earlier

factual_timeline.event: Patient first evaluated by Dr. Dodds

tests_performed.result: Proteinuria with levels of 3.5 grams per day

allegations.allegation: Failure to order UPEP and SPEP tests
